text: Thoreau's economy at Walden was, at its core, an economy of attention. He calculated that a laborer working for wages spent more of his life earning the money to pay for his house than a man who built a simple shelter with his own hands in a few weeks. The same logic applied to the mind: every possession beyond necessity required mental maintenance — insurance, storage, repair, worry. Philosophers since Diogenes had warned that objects possess their owners as surely as owners possess objects. Thoreau made this insight operational, reducing his possessions to a few tools so that his hours could be spent noticing rather than managing.
question: What conclusion did Thoreau draw about possessions and mental attention?

options.text: Owning tools is the only ethical form of property
Every possession beyond necessity requires mental maintenance
Physical labor frees the mind from abstract distraction
Diogenes rejected all tools, not only luxuries
